65.title: Tampering with Computer Source Documents
65.summary: Whoever knowingly or intentionally conceals, destroys, or alters any computer source code required by law shall be punishable with imprisonment up to three years, or fine up to two lakh rupees, or both.
65.punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine up to ₹2,00,000 or both
65.relevance: Applies when source code or electronic records are tampered with during investigation

65B.title: Admissibility of Electronic Records
65B.summary: Electronic records shall be admissible as evidence if: (1) produced by a computer in regular use, (2) information was regularly fed into the computer, (3) the computer was operating properly, (4) the duplicate is a reproduction of the original. A certificate identifying the electronic record must be signed by a senior person responsible for the computer.
65B.punishment: Not a criminal section — governs evidence admissibility procedures
65B.relevance: Critical for ensuring digital evidence is court-admissible. Every forensic examiner must follow 65B procedures.
certificate_requirements: State the device was operating properly
Identify the person who signed (responsible officer)

66.title: Computer Related Offences
66.summary: If any person dishonestly or fraudulently performs any act referred to in Section 43 (unauthorized access, damage, virus introduction, etc.), they shall be punishable with imprisonment up to three years or fine up to five lakh rupees or both.
66.punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine up to ₹5,00,000 or both
66.relevance: Primary section for prosecuting hacking, unauthorized access, and data theft

66A.title: Punishment for Sending Offensive Messages (STRUCK DOWN)
66A.summary: This section was struck down by the Supreme Court in Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015) as unconstitutional for violating Article 19(1)(a) — freedom of speech.
66A.punishment: Section declared unconstitutional — no longer applicable
66A.relevance: Important legal precedent for understanding limits of cyber law

66B.title: Punishment for Dishonestly Receiving Stolen Computer Resource
66B.summary: Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen computer resource or communication device shall be punished.
66B.punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine up to ₹1,00,000 or both
66B.relevance: Applies to possession of stolen devices or data

66C.title: Punishment for Identity Theft
66C.summary: Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly makes use of electronic signature, password, or any other unique identification feature of any other person shall be punished.
66C.punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine up to ₹1,00,000
66C.relevance: Applies to cases of identity theft, credential stuffing, and phishing

66D.title: Punishment for Cheating by Personation Using Computer Resource
66D.summary: Whoever by means of any communication device or computer resource cheats by personation shall be punished.
66D.punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine up to ₹1,00,000
66D.relevance: Covers social engineering, CEO fraud, and online impersonation

66E.title: Punishment for Violation of Privacy
66E.summary: Whoever intentionally or knowingly captures, publishes, or transmits the image of a private area of any person without their consent shall be punished.
66E.punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine up to ₹2,00,000 or both
66E.relevance: Privacy violation cases involving unauthorized surveillance

66F.title: Punishment for Cyber Terrorism
66F.summary: Whoever with intent to threaten unity, integrity, or sovereignty of India accesses a computer resource without authorization and causes significant harm shall be punished.
66F.punishment: Imprisonment which may extend to imprisonment for life
66F.relevance: Most severe cyber offence — applies to state-sponsored attacks and critical infrastructure targeting

67.title: Punishment for Publishing Obscene Material
67.summary: Whoever publishes or transmits obscene material in electronic form shall be punished.
67.punishment: First conviction: up to 3 years + ₹5,00,000. Subsequent: up to 5 years + ₹10,00,000
67.relevance: Applies to CSAM investigations and obscene content distribution

70.title: Protected System
70.summary: The appropriate Government may declare any computer resource essential to Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) as a Protected System. Unauthorized access to a Protected System is punishable.
70.punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine
70.relevance: Critical infrastructure protection — SCADA, power grids, financial systems

72.title: Breach of Confidentiality and Privacy
72.summary: Any person who has secured access to any electronic record, book, register, correspondence, information, document or other material under this Act and discloses it to any other person without consent shall be punished.
72.punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years or fine up to ₹1,00,000 or both
72.relevance: Investigator liability — forensic examiners must maintain confidentiality

chain_of_custody_checklist: Document the date, time, and location of evidence collection
Record the name and designation of the collecting officer
Photograph the evidence in situ before touching or moving it
Use write-blockers before connecting any storage media for examination
Create a forensic image (bit-by-bit copy) of the original media
Generate and record hash values of evidence at collection time
Seal original evidence in tamper-evident bags with unique identifiers
Maintain a continuous log of every person who handles the evidence
Store evidence in a secure, access-controlled environment
Document every analysis step performed on the forensic copy
evidence_handling_guidelines: DO: Use write-blockers to prevent accidental modification of evidence
DO: Work only on forensic copies, never on original evidence
DO: Document every action performed during analysis
DO: Maintain separate chain-of-custody forms for each evidence item
DO: Use forensically sound tools certified for court proceedings
DON'T: Power on a suspect computer without proper documentation
DON'T: Connect storage devices without write-protection
DON'T: Share evidence data without proper authorization
DON'T: Delete, modify, or overwrite any files on original evidence
DON'T: Use the suspect's computer to browse its contents directly
admissibility_tips: Ensure all electronic evidence complies with Section 65B of the IT Act
Prepare a 65B certificate signed by a person in charge of the computer
Document the relevance and authenticity of each piece of digital evidence
Maintain an unbroken chain of custody from seizure to court presentation
Use internationally recognized forensic standards (ISO 27037, NIST 800-86)

key_precedents.case: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015)
key_precedents.summary: Supreme Court struck down Section 66A as unconstitutional. Landmark ruling on free speech in cyberspace.
Supreme Court ruled that electronic evidence without Section 65B certificate is inadmissible. Overruled Navjot Sandhu case.
key_precedents.relevance: 65B certificate is mandatory for digital evidence admissibility

key_precedents.case: Shafhi Mohammad v. State of Himachal Pradesh (2018)
key_precedents.summary: Supreme Court softened the Anvar ruling — 65B certificate requirements may be relaxed when the original device owner cannot produce it.

key_precedents.case: State of NCT of Delhi v. Navjot Sandhu (2005)
key_precedents.summary: Allowed call records as electronic evidence. Later partially overruled by Anvar on 65B requirements.
key_precedents.relevance: Historical context for electronic evidence jurisprudence

key_precedents.summary: Email correspondence accepted as electronic evidence under Section 65B.
